Good afternoon from China!

JMG, I don't know whether you know John Beckett, or follow his Patheos blog, but he has been posting for some time about Gods claiming individuals to work for Them; his latest post on this is here: https://www.patheos.com/blogs/johnbeckett/2019/11/sometimes-the-gods-take-what-they-want.html I myself had an incredibly intense, I might say life-changing, experience of this last week.

His observation is that the Veil between the worlds is thinning, and the Old Gods are finding it easier to reconnect with us. Where this overlaps with your own areas is the idea of Tower Time: that the cheap energy of the last couple of centuries is coming to an end; the foundations of our Tower (aka our economy, society, the religion of Progress) are being washed away, and the time is approaching when the Tower will fall.

In his latest post, John talks about how he knows the Gods are virtuous, yet he sees them behaving badly, and how he struggles to reconcile this. Another commenter points out that we mortals cannot comprehend the Gods' view of virtue, and I've added that perhaps we should look at the virtues that prevailed in the societies in which these Deities were mainstream.

In the Celtic and Norse cases, for example, these were tribal, aristocratic, warrior societies, with the values which that would entail.

I wonder what you think of this in general and, in particular, how it relates to your own ongoing discussion of the breakdown of unitary states, the rise of proto-warbands, and the likelihood of full-blown warbands in the future. Is the rise of these values in this world making it easier for old gods which share those values to return?

Bogatyr

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I don't follow Beckett -- he was a student of mine once, and we didn't part on good terms -- but even a broken clock, etc. I'd comment, first, that if we say "the gods are virtuous" we have to start with an understanding of virtue very different from the one that's been stuck sideways in the western mind for a good many centuries now. The word "virtue" literally means "excellence" -- that's why people used to speak of the virtues of plants and stones; they meant the excellences of those plants and stones, the things for which they are potent and effective. As Alasdair MacIntyre pointed out in his extremely useful book After Virtue, the virtues are independent; you can (and usually do) have some and not others, and despite all the ingenuity of monotheists, no being anywhere can have all of them, because they routinely conflict with one another.

Each god and goddess exemplifies a virtue, or a group of closely related virtues. Thus Mars, say, embodies the warrior virtues. There are plenty of virtues he doesn't have anything to do with, because the warrior virtues are incompatible with a lot of other virtues. Fertility deities embody the virtue (i.e., excellence) of reproductive abundance; they're not virtuous at all in the monotheist sense of the word, because that's not their job. No god expresses all virtues, but that's fine, since there are many gods; whatever virtue or set of virtues you make central to your life, there's a god or a goddess who embodies it.

Right now we're living through the end of our civilization's Age of Reason and watching the first stirrings of the age that normally follows it: the Age of Memory, as I've called it. One consequence of that shift is that it's becoming easier for a lot of people to experience the presence of the gods; this is what drives what Spengler calls the Second Religiosity, the renewal of religion that always follows an Age of Reason, and redefines religion in ways that make sense to a postrational society. Another consequence is that our civilization is beginning to come slowly apart in the usual way, and the rise of warband culture and the associated virtues are a normal part of that. Are these connected? No doubt, but the connections are complex and subtle.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: No, the Yugas are on a much longer scale. According to the Surya Sidddhanta and other classic Hindu sources, the Kali Yuga began in 3102 BC. There's a great deal of disagreement about how long it will run; apparently one set of calculations gives a length of 12,000 years for the entire cycle of yugas, in which case we're either in the last stages of the Kali Yuga or the very first stages of the next Yuga. There are other sets of calculations that give it a much longer duration, though, so don't hold your breath -- and if I understand correctly, after the Kali Yuga comes an ascending cycle through the Yugas, and the Dvapara Yuga (the next one) is better than the Kali Yuga but far from Utopia!

comments.title: Yet Another Geomancy Question
comments.content:
I felt a weird and toxic energy on me during much of the day, and it was strong enough that it would seem to "jump" to my wife-- she would be near me and feel agitated, tired or in physical pain. A couple of days ago I got a dental infection, and it seems to have co-incided with that-- with the sense of weird energy accompanying severe tooth pain etc.

I chased it off, at least for the time being, with a hoodoo bath followed by the OIW rite (the latter proceeded by a divination to make sure it was safe to do in the same house as my pregnant wife.) I then did a house chart on the question, "Is this weird energy due to a curse or astral attack of some kind?"

The results have Cauda Draconis in the 1st house and Puer in the 12th. The chart is perfected by Albus in the 2nd and 11th houses. Puer also jumps to the 9th house, so the chart is also perfected by aspect.

A couple of questions--

1. This is the second or third geomancy chart relating to a sense of hostile, outside energy I've done which has been perfected by Albus. My sense is that it regularly indicates a person obsessing about me, which I then perceive and feel due either to the person's natural psychic ability, my own hypersensitivity, or a combination of both. Is this your read on it also?


2. The quesited jumps to the 9th house, as I said, and perfection is accomplished by translation via the 2nd and 11th houses. I am not sure what to make of the 2nd, except that I've had to turn down work due to the dental infection, so it has impacted my money. But what about the presence of the 9th and the 11th? The 9th suggests religion, but I'm not an active member of any church. (I am technically a baptized Catholic.) I participate in an online group for religious occultists, and I used to regularly attend 12 step meetings. There are very spiritual people that I respect a great deal that participate in both of these things, but both also attract people whose energy is... stunningly toxic. Could it be that a member of one of these groups is sending nasty energy my way, deliberately or not?

3. Or is there something else I'm not seeing?

4. (I don't pay much attention to the Judges and Witness in a house chart, but those are RW: Laetitia, LW: Cauda Draconis, J: Conjunctio, if that helps.)

replies.title: Re: Yet Another Geomancy Question
replies.content:
1) It's quite possible that Albus is trying to tell you something about the person who's responsible, and so yes, that's a reasonable interpretation.

2) Yes, that's very likely, and in fact Albus might indicate that someone is praying for (or, rather, at) you, thinking that they're acting out of good intentions.

In your place I'd get busy with ritual magic. Do you have enough confidence in your ability to create protective amulets, consecrate them, and place them in the rooms where you and your wife spend most of your time? That would be one helpful strategy.

Here are just six of the benefits that transfer (of the 12 Great Vows Medicine Buddha made). These are benefits one is setting up to have in future reincarnations. If one's merit is also good in this lifetime you can receive some of them even in the current one (Saints, Yogis, and Mystics can receive them all in their current life). The following are just a handful of the extensive benefits one receives if one enters Samadhi (a type of single-pointed, gentle, focused attention) while listening to the sound of Medicine Buddha's name. 1. First Great Vow

I vow that in a future life, when I have attained Supreme, Perfect Enlightenment, brilliant rays will shine forth from my body, illuminating infinite,countless boundless realms. This body will be adorned with the Thirty-Two Marks of Greatness and Eighty Auspicious Characteristics. Furthermore, I will enable all sentient beings to become just like me. 3.Third Great Vow

I vow that in a future life, when I have attained Supreme Enlightenment, I will, with infinite wisdom and skillful means, provide all sentient beings with an inexhaustible quantity of goods to meet their material needs. They will never want for anything.


4. Sixth Great Vow

I vow that in a future life, when I have attained Supreme Enlightenment, sentient beings with imperfect bodies – whose senses are deficient, who are ugly, stupid, blind, deaf, mute, crippled, hunchbacked, leprous, insane or suffering from various other illnesses – will, upon hearing my name (ie. when in meditation), acquire well-formed bodies, endowed with intelligence, with all senses intact. I vow that in a future life, when I have attained Supreme Enlightenment, sentient beings afflicted with various illnesses, with no one to help them, nowhere to turn, no physicians, no medicine, no family, no home – who are destitute and miserable – will, as soon as my name passes through their ears (ditto above note), be relieved of all their illnesses. With mind and body peaceful and contented, they will enjoy home, family and property in abundance and eventually realize Unsurpassed Supreme Enlightenment.


6. Tenth Great Vow

I vow that in a future life, when I have attained Supreme Enlightenment, those sentient beings who are shackled, beaten, imprisoned, condemned to death or otherwise subjected to countless miseries and humiliations by royal decree – and who are suffering in body and mind from this oppression – need only hear my name (ditto above note) to be freed from all these afflictions, thanks to the awesome power of my merits and virtues.

comments.title: Initiation, Theogony
comments.content:
I wanted to share an interesting experience I had lately.

A couple of weeks ago I wrote about how I have, for many years, bounced between different religious traditions, unable to keep to one, and how a Geomancy chart seemed to indicate that this is just kind of where I'm at right now. (Puer in 9 in response to "If there is a religious tradition for me, what planet is it under?) You responded that Syncretism is a time-honored tradition, and why not just accept that that's what I do and own it?

Well, first I wanted to thank you for that. It's not like I've made any actual change in response to you saying that-- I've kept my hybrid Druid-Taoist-Catholic-Hellenist practice the way it is, except I've stopped guilt-tripping myself for it!

But something interesting happened recently that I wanted to share. For the last month or so, I've been lighting an appropriate candle and singing an appropriate Orphic hymn every day. The result has been that the planetary deities have been manifesting very strongly and clearly in my life, which has been wonderful. I took up the practice in order to connect more clearly to astrology, but it's gone well beyond that now. I've started obsessively reading the literature of ancient Greece and Rome, and it's clearer and easier for me than ever before! I used to struggle to get through a page of Aristotle; now he's a joy. I've been devouring Gibbon and youtube lectures on Plotinus and Neoplatonism.

And then a couple of days ago I picked up the copy of Hesiod that's been on my shelf for, literally, years. For some reason I just couldn't get through a sentence of it. Now-- Well, I devoured the Theogony in an evening and had to force myself to stop and go slowly through Works and Days. It's not just that I am enjoying them. It's that-- You've talked about how a work of fiction can function as an initiation, and cited Dion Fortune's work in this regard. Reading the Theogony was an initiatic experience, as powerful as meditating through the Celtic Tree of Life. It wasn't like I was unfamiliar with Greek myth before this. As a child my favorite book was a giant collection of Greek myths. But now I'm seeing things-- but not just seeing, perceiving, under-standing-- for the first time. In Hesiod's world, the Sky is a living being, and so is Night, and so is Love, and so is Oath, and so are Wealth and Poverty. Everything one can encounter-- natural phenomena, cultural phenomena, human emotions, human experiences-- all of these are living Powers with whom one can relate. Today's weather and the authority of the king are both the presence of a living power named Zeus. Demeter gives forth the holy grains; grapes are the gift of Bacchus. Rivers are living beings, the children of the ocean. Night is the mother of Dreams and Sleep, and Nemesis, and the Fates and the Furies, and Oath who I mentioned before-- and reading, one feels that none of this is a metaphor, but simply the way things are.

As I read, I continually feel: This is the world that I want to live in. This is the living world, enchanted, sacred, and above all else, inhabited. And it's so different-- utterly and completely-- from the Modern world, or even the Christian world that preceded the Modern world. This is the Living World. And it's the only world I want to live in!

comments.title: W B Yeats
comments.content: A somewhat "scholarly" question. I´ve heard (on an YouTube clip called "The Irish Magical Tradition" at OpenSourceOccultTV) that the Golden Dawn got their ideas from an Anglo-Irish magical tradition popular among the Protestant Ascendancy, including W B Yeats. Is there any truth to this? Yeats was a member of the GD, but didn´t the GD get their ideas from the "normal" Hermetic tradítion, or was this tradition particularly strong in Ireland?

replies.title: Re: W B Yeats
replies.content:
I've never seen a scrap of evidence to support the claim that Yeats had any contact with an Anglo-Irish magical tradition, and huge amounts of evidence that conflict with that claim. His father was a rationalist atheist, and as a young man Yeats got involved in Hermeticism and the Theosophical Society precisely because he wanted a connection to spirituality he didn't get from his upbringing -- he wrote about that at quite some length in some of his more autobiographical essays, and also his letters. That led him to the Golden Dawn, which he joined in 1890, three years after the order was founded -- so he certainly wasn't around to help found it, and I don't recall any of the other early members coming from Anglo-Irish circles (though I could be wrong here).

The Golden Dawn's origins, rather, are straight out of the English Hermetic movement with some French additions. The cipher manuscripts were originally written by Kenneth Mackenzie -- Darcy Kuntz demonstrated that some years ago in a fine bit of research -- and drew on material that had been circulating in the London occult scene since the 17th century.

comments.title: occultism and health
comments.content:
Dear John,

this is the first time I have contacted you although I started reading your blog more than ten years ago. Since then you have been part of my life. Thank you for helping me understand the world and my life better.

What made me write is that I had a dream about you which I interpreted as a nudge. By the way, I know you are good at languages, but I don’t suppose you can speak Czech, my mother tongue. In the dream, however, you could say simple sentences, which made me feel delighted. Hardly anybody learns to speak Czech ;-)

I would like to ask whether practising magic requires good health. I suffer from a kind of autoimmune disease close to lupus, but in a very mild form. The most unpleasant symptoms are weakness and fatigue. There are better times and there are worse. Now I am slowly crawling out of one of the worse phases.

My major textbooks are your The Druidry Handbook and Ogam: The Celtic Oracle of the Trees by Paul Rhys Mountfort that you recommended. I try to do the exercises, practise meditation (mostly on ogham fews) and ogham divination, but to be honest, I sometimes skip, not always due to tiredness. Even so, ogham seems to be working for me. I asked it whether I should do more about occultism, for example start studying The Cosmic Doctrine with you, I asked other questions too, but most of the answers seem to be pointing in the same direction: get stronger, pay attention to your body, seek healing and don’t try to push too hard. At least that is my interpretation.

And yet I don’t want to give up. Do you think it is possible to stay with the current practice and add more when I feel up to it? I do not know where exactly my limits are. That’s the thing with boundaries that you learn where they are only when you cross them. I don’t want to get too far behind, but I don’t want to be too cautious either. On the other hand, could it be magic that might help me to get better health?

In some Czech fairy tales there is a character of an old woman living outside a village, who is a herbalist, very wise and kind. The princesses didn’t appeal to me much, I projected myself on this woman. I am 43 now, married, but without children (not willingly). I think I still may have enough time to get closer to my model. If nothing else, I may be able to grow old ;-)

Hana

replies.title: Re: occultism and health
replies.content:
Hi Hana,
I have something that sounds the same as what you have. I started studying and practicing from the Druidry Handbook and the Druid Magic Handbook about five years ago. I have often been blocked from going as fast as I like in my studies by my divinations which were uniformly negative for the first two years. I finally showed my cards to my sister and asked what they were about. She said, "It is about your health, Dolly." I had thought it was something spiritual...

So, I started taking better care of my health and my cards and magic practice both got better. As for knowing what our limits are, I find it safe to assume I can only do about 50% of what i think I can do. You might also try one up one down as a way to get your health better. This means, one hour up doing things followed by one hour down resting flat on your back. You can read or pet the cat for that hour but nothing more strenuous.

In all, I have found my magic studies a great boost to my health, happiness and peace of mind. I wish you the best in your journey.
Maxine

replies.title: Re: occultism and health
replies.content:
No, I've never gotten around to learning Czech, but you've inspired me to go look up a few basic phrases. I studied Russian in high school, so the Slavic language family isn't too foreign to me -- and the fact that few people learn Czech just makes it more interesting to me. ;-)

My wife also has an autoimmune disease, though a different one, and practices magic, so I asked her for her advice. Her response was that you can certainly practice magic, but it's important to pay attention to your body and not push yourself. If a practice makes you feel stronger and healthier, stay with it; if it makes you feel tired now and again, work out when you should do it and when you shouldn't; and if it always makes you feel tired and weak, don't do it.

One of the effects of autoimmune diseases is that they make the aura weak and prone to damage of various kinds, including leakage; that implies that you'll need to be more than usually careful with magic. Still, if you pace yourself and don't push beyond your strength, you should be fine.

It turned out that the source of the water was a simple leak in my home's supply line. I had been misinformed about where that line runs.

From various clues, it appears the line was leaking since before I moved here, over a year ago. Possibly much longer than that. Gallons per minute had been soaking silently into the sand, until October's heavy rains raised the water table and forced the flow to the surface. (Water is only metered when it reaches the house, so the town water department has no easy way to detect such a situation, nor do they seem particularly concerned about it as long as the water stays underground! That also means there's no financial hit for me either, except I paid a pro to fix the leak, after doing the digging myself. But to someone accustomed not to flush "on yellow" and similar practices, the waste is on a scale hard to fathom. And a reminder that waste on even larger scales is tolerated too.)

Afterward, I realized none of this means nature spirits weren't involved, or that I can neatly sort the incident into a "mundane, not magical" category. We (present company most likely excepted) seem to think water is truly expressing its elemental nature only when it bursts out of pipes and riverbanks and floods something, not the other 99.9% of the time when it's flowing orderly through those rivers and pipes. But that can't be true.

Here's how this might be relevant. When I address the entities that exist here, I've been starting with a single basic question: "What/Who is here?" I make an effort to use all my senses, perceptions, and such comprehension of different time scales as I can manage, to receive the answers. I don't want to exclude anything/anyone too obvious ("swans") or too subtle ("the change of seasons") as long as I can actually perceive them at least indirectly ("microbes"). And I've been doing this for the better part of a year now.

Yet, a railroad is here, across the river, out of sight but easily audible at times, and I've been disregarding it. A water treatment plant is here, directly adjacent, also out of sight but once in a while (fortunately not too often) making its presence known via other senses, and I've been disregarding it. Not to mention my own house, which I don't (and can't) disregard in daily life and doesn't disappear when I ask "Who's here?" but I've been acting as if it does.

It's not very likely that Railroad will end up standing for one of the elements in my SoP, but my intention is to learn about nature spirituality (in however rudimentary a way) from the world with a minimum of preconceptions. (This isn't ancient Greece, or Scandinavia, or Mesopotamia, and the local natives' pre-colonial religion hasn't survived.) So, realizing one huge preconception I'd overlooked might be a step forward.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Okay, first of all, you get that this is fiction, right? The ritual was based on standard devotional rituals from a variety of sources, but you probably won't get the kind of fast results Jenny did -- well, unless you're also the hybrid child of a human mother and one of the Great Old Ones. ;-)

The ritual is devotional rather than magical, so it doesn't include a formal opening of sacred space, a banishing, or the like. Lighting a candle or incense for devotional practice is very common across many religions; the potpourri was simply what came out of my imagination as I worked on the scene. Yes, the oil is an essential oil blend; it contains attar of roses, frankincense oil, and cinnamon leaf oil -- probably a bunch of other things as well, but that's what I imagined.

The words "Ia, Ia, G'noth-ykagga-ha, Ia, Ia, Tsathoggua" came from a classic Cthulhu mythos story -- I don't currently remember which one -- and I doubt anybody, including the original author, had or has the least idea what it means; I used it as the equivalent of a short prayer or a mantra. (In the Japanese esoteric Buddhist tradition I know best, there's a mantra in garbled Sanskrit for each of the deities, and you recite that before an image of the deity before prayer or meditation.) What follows in the middle isn't meditation, it's a specific kind of prayer -- the kind the old authors used to call "the prayer of simple regard," where instead of talking to a deity, you attend to the deity, as though listening for a whisper.

Could you use this format for an actual devotional practice, done morning and evening? Of course. Simply come up with a potpourri mix and essential oil blend suited to the deity, work up a short prayer to the deity in place of the Aklo incantation, and give it a try.

comments.title: etheric insulator materials and triplicates
comments.content:
Greetings John.

I found it quite shocking last week that one should not use silk or linen for a protective talisman. Wondering about possible principles behind this prohibition, I remembered that it is usual to keep one's divinatory cards wrapped in silk - and likewise I do this with geomancy stones. This is presumably to keep them shielded from etheric influence.

Do I rightly conclude that some quality of silk and of linen acts to block etheric energy? If so, is it known what other common materials do this? F'r instance, if I can't usefully keep the salt and nail in a silk pouch, I'm inclined to use leather but don't know if that will likewise prevent the amulet's working. And if one were to wear the materials in question might that have effects on the person's exposure to and tranmsission of such energy?

A couple of other questions, if I may. In a shield chart concerning the relationship between me and another person, would the triplicities refer to me, to that other person, or give a combined picture?
Further, do you read triplicities in the same way as mini Witness and Judge sets, as depicting causal relationships between the matters the figures represent, freeform acording to intuition or otherwise? It isn't clear to me how rigidly structured these groups of figures and their referents are.

Thanks and good wishes

kallianeira.

replies.title: Re: etheric insulator materials and triplicates
replies.content:
1) Silk and linen are the standard etheric insulators because most other substances don't have the same properties. Cotton is the standard cloth these days for amulets, because it's cheap and doesn't block etheric energies, but leather should be fine as well.

2) I normally use house charts for issues involving more than one person, precisely because it's easier to figure out who's being discussed. By and large, though, the triplicities refer to the querent alone.

3) That's something you need to work out by practice. I find it best to start using the rules in a fairly rigid way, and lighten up a little at a time, using intuition and then checking to see if the results are accurate.

comments.title: Interesting article
comments.content:
Hope this isn't off-topic, JMG. It's an article I stumbled onto about the legacy of the materialist New Age movement and its attempts to appropriate Zen Buddhism. In a nutshell, the article nailed why I feel so uncomfortable in New Age bookstores. They have always seemed a bit slimy underneath all of the comforting incense smells and comfy furniture and crystals. Considering Keith Raniere and John of God were once esteemed in New Age circles, it's safe to say those circles are highly suspect.

I have a friend who is an employee in a New Age bookstore who says once a rich guy came in and bought out all of the bookstore's statues and furnishings just so he could re-create the environment in his home. Isn't that sad?

The article: http://www.engaged-zen.org/articles/Kobutsu-New_Age_Legacy.html?fbclid=IwAR29TNoTwaDdY5CoTtoaJKO6pMw923V4eja5AXAaMB8r01ECecPva4RXndM

replies.title: Re: Interesting article
replies.content:
The joke in the Pagan community has been for years that the difference between the New Age and Paganism is a decimal point--IOW the tarot class from a Witch is $50, from a New Ager it is $500. I did a bit of research on the more substantive differences years ago for an academic paper. One major difference is that many New Age groups subscribe to the idea that there is a literal New Age either about to begin or invisibly already begun, in which the nature of humanity is changing radically. This can take a rather elitist turn with implications that not everyone will make the grade. Another difference is that absolutist spiritual leaders of various types (gurus, etc.) are more common in the New Age scene. Some teachings are also based on channeled material, supposedly from an ancient sage (the Seth material for example). Doonesbury parodied this wonderfully with ex-cheerleader, Hollywood starlet Boopsie, channeling Hunk-Ra, a barbarian warrior who occasionally manifests when Boobsie is in contract negotiations. A friend of mine, who had been in an all woman's coven with me for over two years in the 1970s later transferred her allegiances to more New Age type feminist spirituality. When she published a spiritual autobiography a few years later I noted that her time as a Witch was completely omitted.

Rita

replies.title: Re: Interesting article
replies.content: Malone's analysis seems spot on to me. The New Age movement, like a lot of pop-culture spiritual movements, started off with high ideals and bright visions, and gradually slid into a grubby marketplace where con artists sold cheap sentiment labeled as spirituality.

comments.title: Yes/no questions in shield chart
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I am exploring how to use shield chart, the way it's described in the Celtic Golden Dawn book, to answer yes/no questions.

As far as I understand it so far, 3 favourable figures in the court is a definite 'yes' and 3 unfavourable is a definite 'no'. 2 of one kind vs 1 of the other mean a less definite 'yes' or 'no' that is further explained by the figures themselves and the rest of the chart.

1. Is this roughly the way to approach it?

2. How would an interpretation go when there is one favourable, one unfavourable, and one neutral figure. For example, RW - Coch (Rubeus), LW - Gwyn (Albus), J - Cyswllt (Conjunctio). I understand that it may depend on the nature of question, so that in a reading answering a yes/no question about love a combination of Mab (Puer) and Merch (Puella) as Witnesses and the resulting Cyswllt (Conjunctio) as the Judge is probably one of the best answers one could wish. Still, there are situations when all three kinds of figures are present - favourable, unfavourable, and neutral. Is it a 'neither' situation rather than 'yes' or 'no' then?

3. Similar to 2 but with the contrarian figure of Ffordd (Via). How does it affect the yes/no interpretation, particularly when it is the Judge?

4. As an example, I've asked a yes/no question recently and it was answered with RW - Llawenydd (Laetitia), LW - Pen y Ddraig (Caput Draconis), J - Ffordd (Via). Does the nature of Ffordd override the rest of the chart and say a clear 'no' in this case? Does it behave as one unfavourable figure with two favourable and then it's a 'yes, but...' kind of answer? Or is it yet something else?

As always, thank you for running Magic Monday. It's one highlight of each week I'm always waiting for! :}

replies.title: Re: Yes/no questions in shield chart
replies.content:
1) Yes, that's correct.

2) In that case, you've got a really mixed bag -- "well, kind of, but not really." Rubeus as the right witness can mean that you're not being honest with yourself about the issue.

3) Via/Ffordd always changes things to their opposites. "Yes and no" is a good translation.

4) It's two good and one neutral. I'd read that specific set as "Yes -- the results won't be what you're expecting, but you'll like them anyway."

comments.title: Need to listen to myself more often and a question after a retreat
comments.content:
Just realized I am late to this MM and just have 20 minutes left! I hope you don't mind a few minutes late, if it is too late when I finish writing I'll save it for next week. Apologize if I'm a tad incoherent, my mind is buzzing and my feelings are going haywire as a consequence of the same things that I know I should not do and a lack of the things that I am reminded time and time again that I should do. Anyway...

I just came back from a 4 day retreat with Sg, it was fantastic to say the least although I was a bit put off several times by the fact that he was too many fans that act like high schoolers at a Back Street Boys concert, or whatever boy band is popular right now. I get it, he is quite awesome and I myself love him and would even call myself a fan but do they really have to throw themselves to his feet despite the fact that he said just five seconds ago to not do it because he finds it disrespectful? The lack of commitment from many of the participants made it hard for me to set myself in the right mood and made me question myself several times if this path is right for me. Still I tried to focus myself on the teaching, meditations and practices rather on the other participants of the program. Despite that, I took with me a lot of insights and great experiences. One that stuck with me the most was that after some processes that we went through, when I got outside barefooted and just stand still on the soil. I could feel quite strongly "energy" crawling up my feet, up my legs and meeting at the Muladhara where it seemed to stop. Then I consciously started to pull it upwards and it would bubble up all the way to the crown of my head. It was fantastic. I had this feeling of the Earth been alive in a way I had never experienced before and since, I seem to be much more aware of the plants, water, etc. and when I touch a stream of a tree I feel a current flowing towards me. Is this what you have referred to as Telluric current? Is this something that I would benefit from repeating? For example, yesterday I started to have the same sensation although it started from the crown of my head instead and I started to try to draw in into my body. It is a funny feeling and as a matter of fact, just by writing about it I am getting it on the top of my head and sometimes it extends to one side of my face, mostly the left side. On my head it feels like a ring, or circle which slowly crawls on a rotating fashion over my scalp. I have also read from a previous MM that it would be beneficial to first draw energy from above. I might be mixing things but as for now, in my understanding it all feels like energy moving in different ways. Could you elaborate on this phenomenon of Telluric currents and energy from above?

Oh, one last thing. I am working on my affirmations and I am stuck with one of them. I tend to be heavily influenced from my surroundings and thought that it would be a good idea to make an affirmation that would help me create a healthy boundary, but I don't want to formulate it in such a way that I get closed off completely since I learn a lot by picking up stuff or that I get fixed into one mindset. Would something like: "I know how to discern what is right for me" be a good formulation?

My best wishes for you, JMG, thank you for another MM!

replies.title: Re: Need to listen to myself more often and a question after a retreat
replies.content:
No, not at all -- you posted this at 7:19pm Eastern time, so you had more than four hours to go!

1) You've just been through an intense experience. Though it's natural to want to hang onto it, you'll be better off if you take some time to ground yourself. Eat solid meals, with animal foods if you can handle those; do chores that get your muscles moving; take care of practical things. That will give the experiences and the empowerment time to settle in and begin transforming your life experience more generally.

2) The solar and telluric currents are the two great flows of life force in our world. The telluric current rises up from the center of the earth, the solar current descends from the heavens -- directly, from the sun; indirectly, from the solar aura, which fills the solar system out to the heliopause. They flow through your etheric body all the time -- but yes, one of the things that can happen as a result of spiritual experiences is that you become aware of those flows.

3) That's workable, but very general. How about "I maintain the boundary between myself and the world in a healthy and balanced way"?

comments.title: Magical Systems and Dryads
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer:

I have two questions this week.

1) A friend of mine is interested in taking up Rosicrucian studies, either with Gareth Knight's "Experience of the Inner Worlds" or Mark Stavish's "Light on the Path;" however, he is also in discernment towards Holy Orders in a sacramental Christian church. I heard from elsewhere that traditional Catholicism/Orthodoxy are magical systems in denial. Assuming this is true in general of traditional sacramental Christianity, should my friend put Rosicrucian studies on the backburner while in seminary (i.e. focus on one system at a time), or is this apples and orangutans?

2) I have a couple of trees on my household's property whose root systems are threatening the foundation of our house. I hate the thought of cutting down and removing them, but it needs to be done. When the time for tree removal comes what could I do to ask the dryads and related spirits to move on before this occurs? What would the best spiritual and ecological approach to this? (Frankly it upsets me that we'd also be displacing the squirrels who hang out with us).

I appreciate any advice on these matters you have in hand, and thank you in advance. Peace and blessings to you and your loved ones.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Magical Systems and Dryads
replies.content:
1) I'd recommend focusing on one set of training at a time. If he puts the appropriate work into preparation for Holy Orders, he's going to be really busy with that, and anything further should wait.

2) As soon as possible, go out and talk to the trees. Let them know what the situation is and tell them you'll give them warning before the cutting takes place. Tell them again the day before the trees are to be felled. Do something with the wood if you can, and arrange to plant at least the same number of other trees somewhere else.

replies.title: Re: language questions

replies.title: Re: language questions
replies.content:
Another linguist with occult training here.  I agree, with a very minor caveat on whether "egregoire" might be the best term for the distinct "personality" of each language.

Your native language does massively influence how you *unreflectively* view the world in which you live.  (Emphasis here on "unreflectively"!  You can always learn to reflect on the unconscious habits of perception your native language has inculcated in you, and thus move beyond their constraints.)  

For the most part one is hardly able to notice how much influence it does exert until one develops a genuine mastery of another language--the less closely related to one's native language, the better.  Or one can learn to notice the same thing through getting a somewhat lower level of mastery of mulitple languages.

Half a century ago the linguist Susan Ervin Tripp did some fascinating work along these lines with children in California who were bilingual in both English and Japanese.  (Tripp herself seems to have had an excellent command of Japanese.)  She interviewed each child on several occasioins, half in English, half in Japanese, using the same questions in each language.  The example I remember best from a talk of hers in 1963/4 was one where the interview question specified a disagreement in the family on some course of action to be taken.  When interviewed in Japanese, the children's answers were along the lines of "It is a very unhappy situation for us all."  When interviewed in English, the answers were along the lines of "I'm going to do what I want anyway."

Well before Tripp's work, in the first few decades of the 20th century, Benjamin Lee Whorf explored the same questions quite profoundly in a series of controversial and ground-breaking articles.  You can find them reprinted--with some unmentioned editorial changes--in Whorf's posthumous selected writings, "Language, Thought and Reality" (MIT Press, 1956).  

Whorf was an esotericist and a member of the Theosophical Society (Adhyar), but also a really solid, insightful scholar whose work was admired by the best academic linguists of his day (notably Sapir and Bloomfield).  (Nor was Whorf the only 20th-century eminent American anthropological linguist with esoteric interests.  John Reed Swanton was another; he was a follower of Emanuel Swedenborg, and also deeply invested in the study of "psychical reserch," as it was called in his day.)
